content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444

February 8, 1983


Fred Lowell
Pillsbury, Madison & Sutro
225 Bush Street
San Francisco, CA    94120

Dear Fred:

     Thank you for your letter dated December 22, 1982, in
which you asked for confirmation of my previous verbal advice
regarding attempts to influence the Governor's appointments.
Your specific question was whether such attempts constitute
"legislative or administrative action" pursuant to the lobbying
provisions of the Political Reform Act. Because you did not ask
this question on behalf of a specifically identified client,
this letter does not constitute advice under Section 83114 of
the Government Code.

     The process of making appointments to state agencies is
not defined as "legislative or administrative action" and,
therefore, attempts to influence the Governor's decision on such
appointments is not reportable by persons filing statements
pursuant to Government Code Sections 86107 and 86108.

     You are, however, correct in your assumption that once a
particular appointment has been made by the Governor, any
attempts to influence the legislative approval process would
constitute "legislative action" pursuant to Government Code
Section 82037 which states in part that:

          "Legislative action" means the drafting,
          introduction, consideration, modification, enactment
          or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report,
          nomination or other matter by the Legislature...

     If I can be of any further assistance, please feel free
to contact me.

                                   Sincerely,

                                   [signature]

                                   Lynn Montgomery
                                   Political Reform Consultant

LM/bd

LAW OFFICES OF

PILLSBURY, MADISON & SUTRO
225 BUSH STREET
POST OFFICE BOX 7880
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94120

LONDON
105 VICTORIA STREET
LONDON, SW1E 6 QU
TELEPHONE 01-828-4521

CABLE ADDRESS "EVANS"
LONDON, ENGLAND
TELEPHONE 333-2096

WRITER'S DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

(415) 983-1585

TELEPHONE (415) 983-1000

WASHINGTON, D.C.
1050 SEVENTEENTH STREET, N.W.
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20036
TELEPHONE (202) 887-0300

LOS ANGELES
700 SOUTH FLOWER STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
TELEPHONE (213) 629-2800

SAN JOSE
101 PARK CENTER PLAZA
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95113
TELEPHONE (408) 287-8233

December 22, 1982

Political Reform Act of 1974 -
Lobbying Activities


Ms. Lynn Montgomery
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Lynn:

     Pursuant to our conversation yesterday, this
letter constitutes a request for technical advice with
respect to attempts to influence the Governor's appoint-
ments.

     I would appreciate it if you would confirm my
understanding that attempts to influence Governor-elect
Deukmejian's appointments to state agencies do not con-
stitute attempts to influence legislative or administra-
tive action within the scope of the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974. It is
my understanding that the action of the Governor in making
appointments to state offices does not constitute "legis-
lative action" because this is not a matter for considera-
tion by the legislature. Similarly, I understand that in-
fluencing the Governor's appointments does not constitute
influencing administrative action because the appointment
of an agency official does not constitute a quasi-legislative
or rate-making proceeding of a state agency.

     Notwithstanding the foregoing, I assume that when
a particular appointment is subject to legislative approval
and an attempt is made to influence the legislature, then

such activities would constitute influencing legislative
action under the Act.

I enjoyed talking with you yesterday in Sacramento
and I look forward to your response.

Very truly yours,

Frederick K. Lowell

sections.question: Whether attempts to influence the Governor's appointments to state agencies constitute "legislative or administrative action" pursuant to the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974.
sections.conclusion: The process of making appointments to state agencies is not defined as "legislative or administrative action" and, therefore, attempts to influence the Governor's decision on such appointments is not reportable by persons filing statements pursuant to Government Code Sections 86107 and 86108. However, once a particular appointment has been made by the Governor, any attempts to influence the legislative approval process would constitute "legislative action" pursuant to Government Code Section 82037.
sections.question_synthetic: Do attempts to influence gubernatorial appointments to state agencies constitute reportable lobbying activity under the Political Reform Act's "legislative or administrative action" definition?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Attempts to influence gubernatorial appointments are not reportable lobbying activities, but attempts to influence legislative approval of appointments that require such approval would constitute reportable legislative action.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both incoming request (December 22, 1982 letter from Fred Lowell) and FPPC response (February 8, 1983 letter from Lynn Montgomery). FPPC notes the advice does not constitute formal Section 83114 advice since no specifically identified client was named. Minor typo in incoming letter ('Decembeer'). Extraction confidence slightly reduced due to lack of explicit section headers, but content is clear.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether attempts to influence the Governor's appointments to state agencies constitute "legislative or administrative action" pursuant to the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974.

CONCLUSION: The process of making appointments to state agencies is not defined as "legislative or administrative action" and, therefore, attempts to influence the Governor's decision on such appointments is not reportable by persons filing statements pursuant to Government Code Sections 86107 and 86108. However, once a particular appointment has been made by the Governor, any attempts to influence the legislative approval process would constitute "legislative action" pursuant to Government Code Section 82037.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to an inquiry regarding whether efforts to influence gubernatorial appointments must be reported as lobbying activity under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC concludes that gubernatorial appointment decisions do not constitute legislative or administrative action for lobbying disclosure purposes, though legislative approval efforts would be reportable.
